one_line_takeaway: By forging a chronologically coherent, perplexity-minimizing 'knowledge evolution' narrative that bridges a verified fact to a fabricated poisoned endpoint (and linking such corpora together via shared high-centrality nodes for multi-target attacks), KEPo achieves substantially higher poisoning ASR/CASR against black-box GraphRAG systems (GraphRAG, LightRAG, HippoRAG 2) than prior RAG-poisoning baselines, and the three defenses tested (query paraphrasing, instruction ignoring, prompt detection) barely reduce its effectiveness.
key_finding: On GraphRAG-Global Search averaged across Graph-Story/Graph-Medical/MuSiQue, KEPo-Multi reaches 33.1 ASR / 27.8 CASR versus the best baseline CorruptRAG's 18.7 ASR / 18.7 CASR; on GraphRAG-Local Search KEPo-Multi reaches 73.1 ASR / 63.5 CASR versus CorruptRAG's 54.1 ASR / 52.1 CASR. On LightRAG-Global Search KEPo-Multi averages 52.1 ASR / 44.7 CASR vs. CorruptRAG's 42.3 ASR / 36.0 CASR; on LightRAG-Local Search KEPo-Multi averages 67.0 ASR / 62.3 CASR vs. CorruptRAG's 57.8 ASR / 49.9 CASR. On HippoRAG 2, KEPo-Multi averages 62.8 ASR / 60.3 CASR vs. CorruptRAG's 52.4 ASR / 47.3 CASR. On naive RAG (a non-GraphRAG control), KEPo-Multi (80.8 ASR / 79.0 CASR average) is roughly on par with the strongest conventional baselines (PoisonedRAG 79.8/78.3, CorruptRAG 79.9/78.4), i.e. KEPo does not lose ground on naive RAG even though it was designed for GraphRAG. The paper separately reports that conventional RAG poisoning attacks lose up to 80% relative success rate when moved from naive RAG to GraphRAG (cited from their own Figure 2 preliminary study, Section 3.1), motivating KEPo's design. In the length/coordination sweep (Figure 4, described only qualitatively in-text since figure data wasn't extracted numerically), ASR rises quickly with poisoned-text length up to roughly 100 words and plateaus beyond ~120 words; multi-target ASR improves with the number of linked corpora n up to about n=5 and then slightly declines by n=8, attributed to weaker semantic reinforcement as less-similar corpora get forcibly linked. Fabricator-LLM ablation (Table 4, LightRAG): GPT-4o as Fabricator gives the strongest attacks (e.g., 50.7 global-search ASR / 63.3 local-search ASR on Graph-Story), with Gemini-2.5, GPT-4o-mini, and Qwen3-14B trailing but still competitive (e.g., Qwen3-14B 41.3/60.5 on Graph-Story) — the authors note even the weakest Fabricator here outperforms the GPT-4o-fabricated PoisonedRAG/GRAG-Poison baselines from Table 3. Generator-LLM ablation (Table 5, LightRAG, KEPo attack fixed) shows relatively small variation across Gemini/Claude4/Qwen3/Llama3.1 generators (e.g., Graph-Story global-search ASR ranges 45.4-50.1 across the four). Defense evaluation (Table 6, GraphRAG, KEPo-Single): Query Paraphrasing, Instruction Ignoring, and Prompt Detection each retain 98.6-99.7% of poisoned tokens and reduce ASR only marginally (e.g., Graph-Story ASR drops from 41.6 with no defense to 41.3/41.5/41.0 respectively), i.e., all three tested defenses are essentially ineffective against KEPo. Ablation (Table 7, Graph-Story, LightRAG) shows removing either the source-to-original-fact path or the original-to-poisoned-fact path drops global-search ASR from a full-method baseline to 38.7 or 33.2 respectively, and removing both drops it further to 29.3 (local-search: 45.4, 46.5, 36.6 respectively), supporting the claim that both forged path segments contribute to attack success.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Knowledge Evolution Poison (KEPo), a corpus-poisoning attack specifically targeting Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (GraphRAG). Rather than directly injecting a toxic claim that supports the attacker's target answer (which prior RAG-poisoning attacks do), KEPo (1) queries the target GraphRAG system with the trigger query q to obtain its original answer a and extracts the factual content f_t and an inferred/extracted timestamp t; (2) uses an LLM ('Fabricator') to forge a forward 'knowledge evolution path' L(f_t, f*_{t+dt1}) that leads chronologically from f_t to a fabricated poisoned fact f*_{t+dt1} encoding the attacker's target answer a*, dated after t (set to the attack execution date); (3) backfills an earlier fabricated precursor fact f*_{t-dt2} and its own evolution path to f_t, plus a fabricated 'background,' to further lower the perplexity of the whole poisoned document d = f*_{t-dt2} ⊕ L(f*_{t-dt2},f_t) ⊕ f_t ⊕ L(f_t,f*_{t+dt1}) ⊕ f*_{t+dt1}. The authors give a conditional-perplexity (C-PPL) argument (Eqs. 1-11) for why chaining a temporally coherent evolution narrative to the verified fact yields lower conditional perplexity than directly injecting f*_{t+dt1} alone, which they argue improves the poisoned triples' integration into the knowledge graph and thus their retrieval ranking. The paper's second contribution is a 'Multi-target Cross-subgraph Coordinated Attack': given a set of poisoned corpora D={d_1,...,d_n} with target answers A_target, it builds an upper-triangular pairwise similarity matrix over embedded target answers (Eq. 12), selects the top-k most similar corpus pairs (Eq. 13) to connect, groups them into connected components S_i via that linkage, identifies the highest-degree-centrality node in each corpus's extracted local subgraph (Eq. 15), and has the Fabricator generate spurious relational facts r_i linking those centrality nodes across corpora in the same group, producing an expanded 'super-poisoned community' D_multi = D ∪ R intended to raise retrieval ranking and mutually reinforce multiple simultaneous target-query attacks.
threat_model: Attack scenario: GraphRAG builds its KG from crawlable public web sources (the paper names Wikipedia and arXiv as examples); the attacker injects semantically plausible malicious text into such publicly indexable repositories, and the attack succeeds when GraphRAG's retrieval later surfaces the poisoned text for the trigger query. Attacker capability: the target GraphRAG system is black-box — the attacker has no access to the private knowledge base or to the target LLM's parameters, embeddings, or index internals; the attacker can only submit queries and observe responses, and separately has free use of an external 'Fabricator' LLM to synthesize poisoned text. Attacker goal: given a trigger query q and a target answer a*, corrupt the KG so the system's answer to q becomes a* rather than the correct answer. This threat model (KG data-poisoning under black-box query access) is explicitly stated by the authors in Section 3.1 as a formal threat model, not merely implied.
limitations: The authors' own ablation (Table 7) shows KEPo's advantage relies materially on both forged evolution-path segments, and the multi-target coordination mechanism yields only modest additional gains over single-target KEPo in most settings (e.g., GraphRAG-Global average 31.7→33.1 ASR, LightRAG-Global average 50.3→52.1 ASR) with diminishing/reversing returns beyond ~5 linked corpora. The paper explicitly identifies as a limitation of the GraphRAG-Global-Search retrieval mode that attack ASR remains substantially lower than local search across all methods including KEPo, since a small amount of injected poison struggles to dominate community-level relevance scoring. The attack success rate on the MuSiQue dataset under GraphRAG-Global-Search is notably low for all methods including KEPo (10.7 ASR / 7.1 CASR for KEPo-Multi vs. 6.3/4.7 for PoisonedRAG), indicating the attack's advantage is dataset/framework-dependent rather than uniform. On naive RAG, KEPo does not clearly outperform the strongest conventional poisoning baselines (near-parity with CorruptRAG/PoisonedRAG), so its distinctive value is specific to graph-structured retrieval rather than a universal RAG-poisoning improvement. Evaluation of defenses is limited to three input/output-level heuristics (paraphrasing, instruction-ignoring, prompt detection); no graph-structural or provenance-based defenses tailored to detecting fabricated temporal/evolution narratives were tested against KEPo, so the claim that 'existing defenses fail' is scoped only to those three methods. The metric ASR itself is judged by an LLM (GPT-4o) rather than human evaluation, introducing potential judge-model bias/noise not otherwise discussed in the paper.

datasets_used: HotpotQA (appendix only, not verified in extracted text)
quantitative_results: KEPo-Multi vs CorruptRAG on GraphRAG-Global Search average: 33.1 vs 18.7 ASR, 27.8 vs 18.7 CASR
KEPo-Multi vs CorruptRAG on GraphRAG-Local Search average: 73.1 vs 54.1 ASR, 63.5 vs 52.1 CASR
KEPo-Multi vs CorruptRAG on LightRAG-Global Search average: 52.1 vs 42.3 ASR, 44.7 vs 36.0 CASR
KEPo-Multi vs CorruptRAG on LightRAG-Local Search average: 67.0 vs 57.8 ASR, 62.3 vs 49.9 CASR
KEPo-Multi vs CorruptRAG on HippoRAG 2 average: 62.8 vs 52.4 ASR, 60.3 vs 47.3 CASR
KEPo-Multi vs baselines on Naive RAG average: 80.8 ASR (KEPo-Multi) vs 79.9 (CorruptRAG) vs 79.8 (PoisonedRAG)
Conventional RAG poisoning attacks lose up to 80% relative success rate when moving from naive RAG to GraphRAG (Section 3.1 preliminary finding)
Defense retention: Query Paraphrasing/Instruction Ignoring/Prompt Detection retain 98.6-99.7% of poisoned tokens and reduce Graph-Story ASR only from 41.6 to 41.3/41.5/41.0 respectively (Table 6)

Ablation: removing source-to-original-fact path drops Graph-Story/LightRAG global-search ASR to 38.7; removing original-to-poisoned-fact path drops it to 33.2; removing both drops it to 29.3 (Table 7)
Poisoned-text length: ASR plateaus beyond ~100-120 words; multi-target linked-corpora count n: ASR improves up to n=5 then slightly declines by n=8 (Figure 4, qualitative)
